title: Build for Strangers
developmental_frame: At 11–12, children can take a build that worked for friends and make it work for people who've never met them: clear instructions, works without anyone explaining, looks trustworthy, and handles mistakes without breaking. This is a genuinely new design skill — empathy for a user you'll never talk to — and a clear step past Q2's iteration with friends. It prepares them to ship to real customers at the launch.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is a thing that works for people who've never met you — the real line between a friend-project and an actual product. When a stranger can pick it up and succeed with no help, you've built something that can stand on its own in the world.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child watches a true stranger — someone who's never seen the thing and can't ask them for help — use their creation successfully. That moment, when it works without the maker in the room, feels like leveling up from 'kid who made a thing' to 'someone who builds real products'. It's a proud, concrete milestone.

artifact: A build made 'stranger-ready': clear instructions, works unaided, looks trustworthy, handles at least one common mistake, and tested by someone who didn't know it beforehand.
success_criteria: The thing can be used by someone with no explanation from the maker.
It guides a first-timer and handles at least one likely mistake gracefully.
A real person who'd never seen it succeeded in using it.

lessons.title: Strangers don't have you to explain
lessons.concept: When friends test your thing, you're standing there to explain. Strangers aren't — they have only what's on the screen or page. So everything a user needs must be built *in*. If it needs you to explain it, it's not ready for strangers.
lessons.real_example: Your quiz made sense because you told your friend 'tap the green one to start'. A stranger sees no green button explained anywhere and is stuck on the first screen. The instruction lived in your mouth, not the product.
lessons.apply_task: Look at your Q2 build and list every place where a stranger would need you to explain something. Those are your gaps.
lessons.reflection_prompt: How many hidden 'you have to explain it' moments did you find? Most builds have more than the maker expects.
lessons.feedback_task: Ask a parent to spot places where your thing assumes the user already knows something.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to someone why a thing that needs the maker present isn't really finished.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: You know your build; imagining a stranger's confusion is your job here. AI can't feel lost the way a real first-timer will — but it can help you write clearer instructions once you've found the gaps.
success_criteria: Places needing explanation are identified across the build.

lessons.title: Clear instructions and signposting
lessons.concept: Guide the first-timer. A short instruction, a clear label, an obvious 'start here' — these signposts let a stranger find their way. Good design quietly tells people what to do next, without a manual.
lessons.real_example: Adding 'Tap START to begin' and numbering the steps turns a confusing screen into one a stranger breezes through. Small signposts, big difference.
lessons.apply_task: Add clear instructions and signposts to fix the gaps you found. Keep them short and obvious.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Could a stranger now get started without asking anyone? Read it as if you'd never seen it.
lessons.feedback_task: Show the updated version to someone and ask if it's now clear where to begin.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show a friend how one good 'start here' signpost helps a first-timer.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI is genuinely helpful for drafting clear, friendly instructions. But test them on a real person — clarity is judged by a confused human, not by how good the words look.
success_criteria: Clear instructions and signposts were added to guide a first-timer.

lessons.title: Handle mistakes gracefully
lessons.concept: Strangers will do unexpected things — tap the wrong button, leave a box blank, get it 'wrong'. A good build expects this and handles it kindly, with a helpful nudge instead of breaking or a scary error. Plan for the mistake.
lessons.real_example: A stranger leaves the name box empty and taps submit. Instead of crashing, your quiz gently says 'Please add your name first'. It caught the mistake and helped, rather than breaking.
lessons.apply_task: Think of one common mistake a stranger might make. Build a graceful response to it (a gentle message or a safe default).
lessons.reflection_prompt: What's the most likely 'wrong' thing a stranger could do? Handling that one well covers a lot.
lessons.feedback_task: Have someone deliberately 'do it wrong' and see if your build handles it kindly.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to someone why good things expect mistakes instead of assuming everyone does it right.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can help you build the graceful response, but keep the Q1 rule — understand every part. And you must decide *which* mistakes matter; that's judgement about your real users.
success_criteria: At least one common user mistake is handled gracefully.

lessons.title: Test with a true stranger
lessons.concept: The real test: someone who has never seen your thing and can't ask you for help. Watch them (silently!) try to use it. If they succeed alone, it's stranger-ready. If they get stuck, you've found the last gaps to fix.
lessons.real_example: You give your quiz to a cousin who's never seen it, say nothing, and watch. They start, play, and finish with no help. That silent success is proof you built for strangers.
lessons.apply_task: Find someone who's never seen your build. Let them try it with zero help while you watch. Note any sticking points and fix the worst one.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Where did the stranger struggle that friends never did? Strangers reveal the truth about your design.
lessons.feedback_task: Ask the stranger afterward: was anything confusing or missing? Take it seriously.
lessons.teach_back_task: Tell someone why a true stranger is the best test of whether a thing is really finished.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Only a real, unfamiliar human can truly test this — AI already 'knows too much' and won't get lost like a real first-timer. Use a real stranger.
success_criteria: A true first-timer used the build unaided.
Remaining gaps were noted and the worst one fixed.
